sentences.text: When they unloaded us from the trucks, we were marched in and separated, men from women.
And we had a selection by Bartenschlager, he was the leader from Majdanek.
He came with a white coat, with a whip, and transferred us into lines, and directed us from right to left, to lines we didn't where we were going.
But I wound up in the line that I was transferred into camp.
Marching into camp, we observed the big chimneys, the terrible stench, the odor.
We couldn't define at that time what the odor was.
But we later found out that the rest of the people were led [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] into the ovens, into the crematoria.
And then they were burned in the crematoriums.
Yes.
We were led into barracks.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was in field five, with rows and rows of barracks.
The barrack consisted of bunks.
There were three and four layers of bunks.
And each one of us was given a bunk with a few more girls.
And they were completely empty planks, no bedding or whatever.
And that's the way we slept on these bunks.
To keep warm, we huddled, one into another, to keep warm at night.
And just like any other camps, were surrounded by lice, a lot of rats.
We were terribly hungry.
Every morning, if it was cold or whatever, we were marched outside.
It was still dark, it was appel.
It was appel to be counted.
Nobody was allowed to be missing.
There was no escape, because the camp was surrounded with electric wires.
Not just one, but between electric wires was a big lake, not to be able to escape.

And a lot of people-- a lot of young girls, like me, not seeing a way, that we'll never survive between the chimneys, and between the hardship, and between the beating that we had daily, a lot of girls committed suicide by throwing themselves on the electric wire.
There was an outhouse by the wires that we used it as toilets.
And a lot of girls, instead to go to the outhouse, they never came back, because they committed suicide.
We never thought that we were going to be liberated from that camp.
Yes, many times.
We never thought that we were going to ever be liberated.
[SIGHS] As a young girl, I had a grandmother living in Zgierz.
That was not far from Lodz.
When I used to go to her for vacation-- and I was a very young child-- she taught me to say Modeh Ani every morning.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And it's maybe now that I am an aged woman-- I somehow believed, being under these circumstances, that every morning I recited Modeh Ani.
And I said [INAUDIBLE] before I went to bed-- to bed, if you could call it a bed.

But somehow the belief in God that I was brought up with, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and being young probably, and the hope that I will still see my parents someday or my brothers, maybe the hope kept me alive.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Even to this day, I never miss Modeh Ani in my life.
I recite this every morning.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] In Majdanek, we worked in fields.
We were marched every morning into fields.
And there was always a whip above your head.
You were not allowed to pick up your head from the work that did.
If you did, you had a whip around your neck.
The same thing when were marching fives out of the camp into fields.
When we were marching fives, God forbid, if somebody made a mistake and jumped into your line as a sixth-- or you never survived, or you had a whip around you.
The SS that used to lead us into field, or the kapo.
No,
Because every day we had different SS, different groups of SS that came, that worked with us.
Bartenschlager, and I remember him vividly.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when we were marching into field, and finish working, we used to walk in by 5-- 6 o'clock, we used to walk into camp, marching into camp.
And we counted.
And then every barrack had a big kettle of food-- of soup.
So were given a ladle of food, of soup.
If you call it soup, it was dirty water.
And we were given a slice of bread.
And this was our ration for the day.
We had no way of washing our self, hot water or anything to keep ourselves clean.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But we lived like animals.
And we worked, and the despair was so great.
And many times we used to wake up with-- people that we used to go to sleep with, and in the morning they were stiff, dead.
And we didn't even have time to mourn them, because we had to go to be counted.
And then on the end, when it was the appel, they used to bring out the dead corpses to be counted.
Because somebody was missing.
So we couldn't be marched out to work, because somebody was missing.
The stubendienst from the barrack.
Every barrack had their stubendienst who used to take care of the barrack and us.
He saw to it that we had to go to sleep on time, the light was out, or something shouldn't go on.
Sometimes they were Jews.
They were selected between the Jewish people.
And many times they were German people.
Not exactly.
Not exactly.
Sometimes we were beaten by them, too.
Well an instance is that when I went to the outhouse and I didn't come back-- so fast away it was required, and I was beaten before I went up to my bunk.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was sleeping with a girl-- a friend of mine that I'm still friends, she lives in New York-- and I went through with her all the camps in Majdanek and all the camps after that, we slept together.
And she has a family now also, living in Israel.
And we always keep in touch.
We're very close.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And one day we were marching from the fields into the camp, and we saw a big sign on the gate.
That whoever wants to submit your number-- because we had no names in camp.
We were going by numbers.
In Majdanek, I have 1204-- that was my number-- pinned on my uniform.
And I had no name in camp, but I had a number.
So it was advertised that whoever wants to submit your number, that they need thousand women to go for a selection to work.
So my girlfriend and I, walking through the gate-- we thought that we have nothing to lose, that we would never come out alive from here anyway.
So we went into the office, and we submitted our number.
That night when I went to sleep on my bunk, the stubendienst, which it was a German at that time, a woman-- came to my bunk and beat me up.
She said to me, why do you have to submit your number?
What, do you think you're going to live?
You are going to a different chimney.
Why is this chimney different than the others?
Why did you have to submit your number?
And called me all kind of names.
But the next morning, whoever submitted their number-- when we came up in the morning to be counted, we were called by our-- by our number, and we were taken to a different barrack.
And we went through for five days a selection.
In that camp, you had no water.
You didn't have a way to keep clean.
You didn't have a way to keep healthy.
But for this selection, all thousand women had to be kept clean, healthy, without any scars on your body.
I really didn't know after the five days that I was selected for that group.
We were given a portion of bread, we were given a shower, and we were given again different clothes than our clothes, than our-- we didn't have proper clothes, but whatever they were giving us.
And we were loaded up into wagons.
Off.
We were completely naked.
The Bartenschlager and a few German women in uniform.
No.
We didn't even know where we are going, or why we are going through the selection.
Not better.
But we were going through certain things, like cleanliness, or picking up our hair, and picking up-- we were in and out investigated for, for ev-- every part of our body was checked.
And after five days, whoever was meant to go for that transport, we were transported with trucks to the trains.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And from the trains, we arrived to Skarzysko.
Yes?
No.
I never saw my father again.
When I arrived in Majdanek, I saw the last time when they-- we were separated.
And that was the last time I saw them.
Because our field, field five, was only women.
We were not-- when we were marching out from the field to work, we were not allowed to even turn our head.
Because many times when we were marching, we saw from faraway, groups of men marching to their destination to go to work.
Or even we saw every morning-- arriving-- trucks and trucks of people, new transport of people to go to the gas chambers.
At that time, we didn't know.
At that time, people that were there longer than I was told us-- or certain kapos, or certain workers-- that they made the selections, they told us.
Like, we observed, when we were taken to the showers, that there were mountains and mountains of clothes, mountains of shoes, mountains of hair.
And people told us that that they make soap from human being.
And we couldn't imagine that this could be [? threw ?].
And we, we saw such big mountains of shoes, of glasses, of books, of clothes from people.
And we couldn't imagine that these people are no more here on this Earth.
I didn't work by the sorting.
As I said, I was working at the field.
But a lot of people were working by the sorting of the clothes.
We couldn't.
Because when you were marching into the camp, after you worked, you were completely frisked to see if you carry something.
If you did, you paid with your life.
So you couldn't take a chance.
No, we didn't.
In fact, after, after we arrived from Majdanek-- when we arrived to Skarzysko, we found out that Majdanek was crossfired and they eliminated the camp.
Because the Russians came near.
And they eliminated the camp and cross-fired all the people.
And all the clothes from Majdanek came to Auschwitz.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because my husband later told me the story-- that they found out, because my husband was in Auschwitz.
And he was working in Birkenau, where all the trains arrive.
And he told me the stories then.
After Majdanek, all the clothes came to Auschwitz.
Not at all.
Not at all.
Yes we did.
They were not allowed.
But certain ones came up, and they were told to get away.
But they only came up, not because feel-- to feel sorry, but to taunt us like animals.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were sorry for us.
The Poles?
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They used to say in Polish to us-- in-- in Polish, it rhymes.
They used to say, before the war it was our-- it was your home and our streets.
And now it's opposite.
It's your streets and our homes.
In Polish they used to say, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [POLISH] And now it's opposite, [POLISH] They used to taunt us that way.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So, but after Majdanek, when we arrived in Skarzysko, it was a-- we found out by arr-- when we arrived-- in fact, in-- in the train when we came from Majdanek to Skarzysko-- Was it a regular train, or was it--
No, cattle trains.
All filled to capacity.
In fact, we were sitting one inserted into other, because that's how far we could survive.
We were in the train by three nights.
No.
And we were there, in the train.
And when we arrived-- and in fact, in our train, in our compartment, there was a girl that said to us-- she was from Warsaw-- and she said that we are going to death, that we will never survive.
We really didn't know where we are going.
And that girl survived the Warsaw ghetto.
And she said that she's going to escape, because we are going to our death.
So she picked a board from the floor and tried to escape.
And the next minute, we-- the train stopped, and the door opened.
And they put us against the wall.
And they said, who was the woman who tried to escape?
And nobody came forward.
So that woman, after a few minutes-- after the SS screamed, if you don't come forward, I'll shoot them all.
She came forward.
And as soon as she came forward, she was shot.
And we were riding the train all the way to Skarzysko.
And we arrived with a dead body in the train, because she tried to escape.
Were you all kind of the same age?
A year or two older or younger.
We were the same age, yes.
They selected all the healthy girls.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when we arrived to Skarzysko, we didn't know the Bartenschlager was there with us, with the transport from Majdanek.
And there was 500 men and 1,000 women arriving to Skarzysko.
Bartenschlager?
He was a very tall man, blonde, with a white coat on.
He mostly looked like a healthy butcher.
And he was wearing very beautiful, polished boots.
And he had a [GERMAN] in his boots-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that he used to hit us with.
A crop.
That they were attached with little stones on the end.
The [GERMAN].
And he had it in the boots and always wore white gloves.
He never touched us, because he had different SS to shoot, or to do the dirty works.
He only-- with his [GERMAN] selected us which way to go.
No.
We never knew his name.
I beg your pardon?
When we came to Skarzysko--
We found out it's an Arbeitslager.
That's an ammunition factory, HASAG.
Because when we went into the gate, there was a big sign, HASAG.
But at that time we didn't know what it stands for.
But we found out it's an ammunition factory.
And people were there, from surrounding area, from the villages.
People that came from their homes.
They came to this factory to work.
They were Jews.
There were no Poles.
There was-- the-- this was a, a working camp, Arbeitslager.
That was not a Konzentrationslager, but a Arbeitslager.
So we were selected to make ammunition for the Germans.
So were given bunks.
We were given a special-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] block.
In fact, our block was marked with a big KL, because it was prepared for us.
And we used to be named [GERMAN], because we came from Majdanek.
The big KL, Konzentrationslager.
And when we got into our barrack, we had given again food, soup, and a portion of bread.
And this was our daily food, again.
Just once a day?
Once a day, we got soup and a portion of bread.
No that was in the evening.
All day we didn't have anything.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] During the day, we had no food.
But we had, given in the morning-- they give us hot coffee.
The hot coffee, we found out later, that it's poisoned.
And in Majdanek the same way.
We had coffee given that it was for the women not to get their period.
And when I had my period, and then it stopped.
Because that coffee, we later found out, was poisoned that we shouldn't have our period.
And I didn't get my period until I was liberated, because my system was all poisoned.
But at Skarzysko, we had the soup and a piece of bread when we marched in from work.
We were 12-hour shift, working.
I was selected to work in [GERMAN].
[GERMAN] is a certain part of the HASAG that made certain bullets, ammunition, for the Germans.
And I was working on a shift-- 12-hour shift, that-- by a table with a lot of women.
We were about 30-35 women making bullets.
Shell.
We had machines that had to fit certain sizes to make the bullet.
The shell had to fit into the bullet-- into the powder-- the powder to go into the bullet.
And I heard a SS woman that was over us-- her name was [GERMAN].
She was just like Bartenschlager.
Tall, fat, and she was very strong, tall woman.
And she used to call me [GERMAN].
I don't know what did she see in me, but I was a good-- supposedly I was a good worker.
And she always used to send with the Polish guy that used to work with us, the manager, that she used to give me an extra piece of bread for working so good.
So in Skarzysko, I was working the ammunition factory.
Skarzysko in 1943.
Beginning '43.
And I worked in Skarzysko in the ammunition factory.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] It's very hard for me to say it.
I was a young girl then, and when we arrived from Majdanek-- there were-- as I said, a lot of people were there from before the war, from surrounding area, that lived in the surrounding area.
That they came from the same city, from Skarzysko.
That they came into the camp, to work there.
A lot of people-- to us it was a novelty, because we came from Majdanek, not having anything.
And a lot of people came from their homes, that they had normal clothes.
And to us it was a novelty.
Over there, I met a young boy, my age.
That he became a friend of mine.
You see, Skarzysko was-- we weren't allowed to be in the same [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] block, to live in-- we were separated, men from women.
But at the time when we went to work in the factory, we were able to mingle men and women.
After the Majdanek, this was more a normal life to us.
Because it was not a concentration camp.
It was an Arbeitslager.
So I met that young fellow.
His name was David.
And he came out from the same town, from Skarzysko.
They were very well-to-do people.
And he was there with his two brothers.
When we arrived from Majdanek, it was an alarm out for his older brother.
He escaped from Skarzysko.
And there was an alarm.
He lives now in Israel.
He was spared.
But I was-- this was my first friend as a young girl.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the day the Russians came near to our camp, and they started-- the Germans started to evacuate the camps-- when they evacuated the camp, the day-- in Skarzysko, they evacuated people into transport.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people went to Mauthausen and all kind of camps.
And we were transported, the whole camp was transported to a-- to Czestochowa, to another Arbeitslager, [?
Orso ?] HASAG.
